[JS Weird World] No.16 Operator Precedence and Associativity
These two terms are a really important thing in JavaScript, and an even more important concept. If you can wrap your head around them, they'll definitely help you out when you're debugging.

Operator precedence basically tells you which operator gets evaluated first. In any given expression you usually don't have just one operator, so JavaScript handles the higher-precedence ones first and works its way down to the lowest.
So what's associativity? Associativity is the order in which operators of the same precedence get evaluated. Usually it's either left-to-right (that's called left-associativity) or right-to-left.

So when an expression has two operators with the same precedence, associativity becomes really important. It's what decides whether you go right-to-left or left-to-right. The course gives a nice example.

Operator precedence and associativity are two really important concepts in JavaScript. They sound dry, but once you actually get them, they help a ton when you're debugging. This post breaks down what each one means, why they matter when two operators share the same precedence, and how to read MDN's precedence table.
